{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"
# Overview of the Service Utilization Assessment Methodology 
Service utilization trend analysis, which identifies the percentage change in service use for each quarter of data compared to the previous quarter.

Disruptions and surplus in service delivery utilization analysis, which helps to identify meaningful positive or negative changes in service use outside of what we would expect given seasonal trends and historical trends.

# Overview of the Service Coverage Estimation Methodology
Coverage estimation analysis uses routine data to estimate trends in service coverage at national and sub-national levels. This occurs by integrating adjusted health service volume data, population projections, and survey data (MICS/DHS). Coverage estimates are calculated for key health indicators using multiple denominator sources, and the optimal denominator is selected by minimizing the error relative to the most recent survey data.

# Overview of the Data Quality Assessment Methodology 
Identifies key data quality issues by assessing indicator completeness, detecting extreme outliers, and evaluating consistency between related indicators.

Applies targeted adjustments to flagged data points by replacing outliers and imputing missing values using a centered 12-month rolling average; fallback facility-level averages are used when insufficient history exists.

Enables sensitivity analysis by producing results under four scenarios (no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and combined), with certain indicators that don't meet inclusion criteria excluded from adjustment. For this analysis, adjustment is for both outliers and completeness."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found in the Annex. All R code and source documentation is also publicly available on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology - HMIS"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Why the two disruption views differ:

- In figure 1, observed values are adjusted for outliers only, so red areas reflect both service drops and reporting gaps.

- In figure 2, observed values are adjusted for completeness + outliers, so red areas reflect service changes only.

# Why both are useful:

- Figure 1 captures the full signal of disruption (service delivery + reporting).

- Figure 2 helps focus on likely true service disruptions — but may mask real declines during stock-outs given that DHIS2 is often not configured to store 0's.

# Question for discussion:

How should we present disruptions going forward — so that we flag both reporting issues and real service changes without losing important signals?","span":4},{"type":"image","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":true,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"imgFile":"PNC_disruption_comparison.png"}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Why Completeness Matters for the Disruptions Analysis"}}

Identifies key data quality issues by assessing indicator completeness, detecting extreme outliers, and evaluating consistency between related indicators — using monthly, facility-level HMIS data.

Applies targeted adjustments to flagged data points by replacing outliers and imputing missing values using a centered 12-month rolling average; fallback facility-level averages are used when insufficient history exists.

# Overview of the Service Coverage Estimation Methodology

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"
# Overview of the Service Utilization Assessment Methodology 
Service utilization trend analysis, which identifies the percentage change in service use for each quarter of data compared to the previous quarter.

Disruptions and surplus in service delivery utilization analysis, which helps to identify meaningful positive or negative changes in service use outside of what we would expect given seasonal trends and historical trends.

# Overview of the Service Coverage Estimation Methodology
Coverage estimation analysis uses routine data to estimate trends in service coverage at national and sub-national levels. This occurs by integrating adjusted health service volume data, population projections, and survey data (MICS/DHS). Coverage estimates are calculated for key health indicators using multiple denominator sources, and the optimal denominator is selected by minimizing the error relative to the most recent survey data.

# Overview of the Data Quality Assessment Methodology 
Identifies key data quality issues by assessing indicator completeness, detecting extreme outliers, and evaluating consistency between related indicators.

Applies targeted adjustments to flagged data points by replacing outliers and imputing missing values using a centered 12-month rolling average; fallback facility-level averages are used when insufficient history exists.

Enables sensitivity analysis by producing results under four scenarios (no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and combined), with certain indicators that don't meet inclusion criteria excluded from adjustment. For this analysis, adjustment is for both outliers and completeness."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"More details on the methodology and data quality adjustment approaches are found in the Annex. All R code and source documentation is also publicly available on GitHub (https://github.com/FASTR-Analytics)."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology - HMIS"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# What is data quality assessment?
The Data Quality Assessment (DQA) methodology applies three core assessments: detection of extreme outliers in reported service volumes, analysis of indicator-specific completeness, and consistency checks between related indicators. For each facility and month, the data are processed to identify anomalies, assess reporting continuity, and validate internal coherence across indicators. Outliers are flagged using Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) methods and proportional thresholds. Completeness is evaluated based on whether facilities report non-zero values across months for core indicators. Consistency is checked across linked indicators (e.g., ANC1 vs ANC4, Penta1 vs Penta3), using predefined ratio thresholds. The results are integrated into a composite DQA score that summarizes whether a facility's data meet all quality benchmarks.

# What is data quality adjustment? 
Data quality adjustment is the process of correcting known issues in routine health data to improve the reliability of analyses. In the FASTR approach, adjustments are applied to HMIS data using automated methods that identify and correct outlier values and impute missing data based on patterns in historical reporting. This allows countries to make more robust use of the data they already have, even when reporting is imperfect.

# Why adjust for data quality?
Routine health data, such as those captured in DHIS2, are an essential resource for monitoring and improving primary health care systems. However, data quality issues — like outliers, incomplete reporting, and inconsistencies between related indicators — can distort analyses and misinform decision-making. 
The FASTR approach includes built-in statistical methods to identify and adjust for these issues, producing more robust and actionable results. While not all data quality challenges can be fully resolved, targeted adjustments improve the reliability of the data and help ensure it can still inform decisions. Rather than treating poor data quality as a barrier, FASTR transforms it into an opportunity for feedback and improvement, enabling countries to use the data they have to make timely, evidence-based decisions.

# How was adjustment done?
The Data Quality Adjustment module uses HMIS data together with the outlier and completeness flags generated by the Data Quality Assessment module, which identify where service volumes are unusually high or missing. Outlier values are replaced with a centered 12-month facility-level rolling average based on valid (non-flagged) data. Missing values due to incomplete reporting are imputed using the same method. If there is not enough valid historical data, a fallback average at the facility level is used instead. Adjustments are applied under four scenarios - no adjustment, outlier-only, completeness-only, and both combined - to enable sensitivity analysis. Indicators which don't meet minimum volume thresholds are excluded from adjustment.
For this analysis, adjustment is for outliers and completeness."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Methodology: Data Quality Adjustment Approach"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"Analysis of routine health management information systems data (HMIS) and the methodological choices contained in this report provide strengths and limitations which are important to consider in the interpretation of the results."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Strengths 
- Leverages routinely collected HMIS data, enabling timely and low-cost monitoring.
- Includes rigorous data quality assessment and adjustment to improve reliability of estimates.
- Enables subnational analysis for granular insights.
- Supports policy-relevant questions through tailored indicator selection and flexible stratification.
- Accounts for seasonality and historical trends using statistical methods not available in DHIS2.
- Complements existing DHIS2 tools and strengthens data use through feedback loops."},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Limitations
- Cannot correct for all types of data quality issues (e.g., systematic underreporting, denominator inaccuracies).
- Does not account for facilities that never report, which may bias results in areas with large reporting gaps.
- Low-volume indicators are more sensitive to anomalies and may not be adjusted.
- Requires consistent indicator definitions and stable reporting systems over time for trend analysis."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Strengths and limitations of this analysis"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Objective and organization of this slide set
This slide set presents a proposed analysis plan template for a country immunization profile for GFF/Gavi partner countries. Data will come from the FASTR HMIS data portal and phone-based health facility assessments (HFA). Somalia is used as an illustrative example.

Each visualization slide includes a description of the objective of the visualization as well as considerations for the visualization and/or analysis for discussion.

The analysis plan is split into three sections:

# Demand
This section includes routine immunization data from the HMIS. Some of these analyses are currently available in the HMIS data portal, others could be available if requested.

# Supply
This section includes findings on immunization service delivery from the HFA.

# Joint immunization data analysis
This section proposes analyses that bring together the demand and supply findings to identify immunization gaps."}]]}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Why the two disruption views differ:

- In figure 1, observed values are adjusted for outliers only, so red areas reflect both service drops and reporting gaps.

- In figure 2, observed values are adjusted for completeness + outliers, so red areas reflect service changes only.

# Why both are useful:

- Figure 1 captures the full signal of disruption (service delivery + reporting).

- Figure 2 helps focus on likely true service disruptions — but may mask real declines during stock-outs given that DHIS2 is often not configured to store 0's.

# Question for discussion:

How should we present disruptions going forward — so that we flag both reporting issues and real service changes without losing important signals?","span":4},{"type":"image","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":true,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"imgFile":"PNC_disruption_comparison.png"}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Why Completeness Matters for the Disruptions Analysis"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# HEALTH FACILITY SURVEY

National and regional representative panel sample of 483 public PHC facilities, including district-level hospitals, health centers, and primary health units (PHUs).​

The sample was stratified by facility type, region, and Gavi prioritization.*​

Note: n corresponds to the total number of surveyed health facilities in each region. The Mudug region was split between Mudug-Galmudug (n = 27) and Mudug-Puntland (n = 19) in the survey sample.​

*Health facilities were categorized as ‘Gavi high priority’ when located in geographic areas characterized by a high prevalence of zero-dose children, defined as children that have not received any routine vaccine. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","subTitleText":"","presenterText":"","dateText":"","logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","smallerSectionText":""},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"primary","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"Analysis of routine health management information systems data (HMIS) and the methodological choices contained in this report provide strengths and limitations which are important to consider in the interpretation of the results."}],[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Strengths 
- Leverages routinely collected HMIS data, enabling timely and low-cost monitoring.
- Includes rigorous data quality assessment and adjustment to improve reliability of estimates.
- Enables subnational analysis for granular insights.
- Supports policy-relevant questions through tailored indicator selection and flexible stratification.
- Accounts for seasonality and historical trends using statistical methods not available in DHIS2.
- Complements existing DHIS2 tools and strengthens data use through feedback loops."},{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"markdown":"# Limitations
- Cannot correct for all types of data quality issues (e.g., systematic underreporting, denominator inaccuracies).
- Does not account for facilities that never report, which may bias results in areas with large reporting gaps.
- Low-volume indicators are more sensitive to anomalies and may not be adjusted.
- Requires consistent indicator definitions and stable reporting systems over time for trend analysis."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Strengths and limitations of this analysis"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":true,"textSize":0.8,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"span":3,"markdown":"# Objective: 
Visualize associations between readiness and coverage

Depending on the country, unit of analysis for this visualization might be different. For countries where we have subnational level representativeness for the HFA data  and subnational coverage data, unit of analysis would be subnational units (as in example).
In other countries, HFA results or coverage data are only representative at national level. In these countries, this analysis would only be exploratory and would be a sampling of HFA facilities from each subnational level and the scatterplot would represent readiness and utilization since we don't have a denominator. {"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1.25,"textBackground":"none","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Objective and organization of this slide set
This slide set presents a proposed analysis plan template for a country immunization profile for GFF/Gavi partner countries. Data will come from the FASTR HMIS data portal and phone-based health facility assessments (HFA). Somalia is used as an illustrative example.

Each visualization slide includes a description of the objective of the visualization as well as considerations for the visualization and/or analysis for discussion.

The analysis plan is split into three main sections:

# Demand
-This section includes routine immunization data from the HMIS. Some of these analyses are currently available in the HMIS data portal, others could be available if requested.

# Supply
-This section includes findings on immunization service delivery from the HFA.

# Joint immunization data analysis
-This section proposes analyses that bring together the demand and supply findings to identify immunization gaps.

Annexes at the end of the analysis plan will describe data collection and analysis methodology"}]]}}
